critique.summary: The article explains strategies for deferring U.S. taxes by reinvesting taxable income into business expenses and leveraging depreciation schedules. It emphasizes that these methods are legal and intended by the tax system to encourage economic growth, while also discussing loan refinancing and inheritance tax implications.
mainPoints: Taxes can be deferred by reinvesting income into business expenses and depreciable assets.
Depreciation schedules can be adjusted to minimize taxable income in profitable years.
Refinancing loans can provide cash without triggering taxable income.
The government incentivizes economic growth through tax policies that reward reinvestment.
Inheritance can reset cost bases, effectively eliminating deferred tax obligations upon death.
Some tax incentives may be exploited as loopholes, but distinguishing legitimate use from abuse is difficult.
critique.truthfulness: The article accurately describes common and legal tax deferral strategies such as depreciation, reinvestment of business expenses, and loan refinancing, which are well-established practices in U.S. tax law. It correctly notes that loans are not taxable income and that inheritance can reset asset cost bases, reducing tax liabilities. However, the article simplifies complex tax rules and omits important legal nuances and risks involved in these strategies. The mention of Modern Monetary Theory is tangential and not directly relevant to tax deferral legality. Overall, the claims are mostly truthful but presented with some informal language and lacking detailed citations.
considerations: The article does not discuss the legal risks and IRS scrutiny associated with aggressive depreciation or loan refinancing strategies.
It omits mention of the Alternative Minimum Tax (AMT) and other tax provisions that can limit deferral benefits.
There is no discussion of the potential consequences of tax evasion versus legal tax avoidance.
The article does not explain the complexity and costs of cost segregation studies or refinancing processes.
It does not address state and local tax variations or international tax implications.
The ethical considerations of tax deferral and avoidance strategies are only briefly mentioned.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers around strategies for deferring or reducing taxable income through business expenses and asset depreciation, with cautionary notes about potential IRS audits. Some comments reflect skepticism about the advice's safety and complexity, while others share personal experiences or related tax system comparisons.

comments.author: jeffreyrogers
comments.text:
Pretty good overview of how/why these deductions reduce your taxable income. Couple of things to note.

Depreciation is recaptured if you sell an asset for more than its depreciated basis. People sometimes get into trouble with this if they rapidly depreciate real estate and then sell it. Even if you sell for less than your purchase price it is possible to owe taxes.

You also aren't going to be able to pay no taxes since you do need to realize some income to pay for mortgage/rent, food, transportation, etc. I guess if you had assets you could borrow against it would be possible to pay for these using the loan proceeds (which are not taxable).
comments.analysis: This comment provides a nuanced explanation of depreciation recapture and the practical necessity of realizing income, reflecting an accurate understanding of tax implications.

comments.author: kg
comments.text:
> Defer US taxes by reinvesting your taxable income into the economy as business expenses, depreciating assets, etc.

Be really careful when doing this. Make sure you have a great accountant - if you go more than a few years without turning a measurable profit, your risk of being audited apparently goes up. My accountant personally cautioned me about this since my business has been in an R&D phase for 5 years so we've been showing a small loss every year. The last thing you want is for the IRS to decide you've been cheating on your taxes.
comments.analysis: This comment offers a practical warning about audit risks associated with sustained losses, which aligns with common IRS scrutiny practices.

comments.author: josefritzishere
comments.text: This feels like a great way to get audited by the IRS. It does not feel like sound advice.
comments.analysis: While brief and somewhat emotional, this comment correctly expresses a common concern about aggressive tax strategies increasing audit risk.

comments.author: dleslie
comments.text: That's a great deal more complicated than our TFSA and RSP programmes, here in Canada.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a useful comparative perspective on tax systems, noting complexity differences between US and Canadian tax-advantaged accounts.

comments.author: hirako2000
comments.text:
I'm not sure to understand how deferring taxes is a better deal than paying it here and now.

Since I'm not a financial adviser, someone asked me take on which 4k projector to buy last Xmas.

I explained that the tech has improved so much lately, they've become somewhat affordable, I recommended a model and pointed ou that he would certainly get a better device next Xmas, for half the price. I thought he would follow suit given his budget was a bit below the retail price. That would just wait.

His response was he would rather go ahead and up the budget a few hundred dollars to get it right away. That projectors will surely get much better by next year, but that he, certainly, will not.
comments.analysis: This comment raises a valid question about the benefits of tax deferral and uses an analogy to illustrate preferences for immediate versus delayed gratification.

comments.author: WarmWash
comments.text: If what was supposed to be your tax dollars is instead going towards giving more people work to do (and hence generate more taxes) the government will be happy.
comments.analysis: This comment insightfully points out the economic rationale behind reinvesting income to stimulate economic activity and potentially increase tax revenue.

comments.author: davidfekke
comments.text: Is this advice from Wesley Snipes?
comments.analysis: This comment is a sarcastic reference to a celebrity known for tax issues, adding little substantive value to the discussion.

comments.author: jimt1234
comments.analysis: This comment is a simple recommendation without explanation or critique, offering minimal analytical content.

comments.author: uoflcards22
comments.analysis: This brief comment expresses enthusiasm but contributes no meaningful insight or analysis.
